verses.text: Hezekiah tears his clothes, goes to the house of the LORD, and sends to Isaiah, calling it a day of trouble and asking prayer for the remnant. He turns to God and His prophet. The right response to blasphemous threats is humble prayer.

Hezekiah tears his clothes, goes to the house of the LORD, and sends to Isaiah, calling it a day of trouble and asking prayer for the remnant. He turns to God and His prophet. The right response to blasphemous threats is humble prayer.

Hezekiah tears his clothes, goes to the house of the LORD, and sends to Isaiah, calling it a day of trouble and asking prayer for the remnant. He turns to God and His prophet. The right response to blasphemous threats is humble prayer.

Hezekiah tears his clothes, goes to the house of the LORD, and sends to Isaiah, calling it a day of trouble and asking prayer for the remnant. He turns to God and His prophet. The right response to blasphemous threats is humble prayer.

Isaiah answers, 'Be not afraid'; God will send a blast upon the king, who shall hear a rumour and fall by the sword in his own land. Deliverance is promised. God can scatter the mightiest army with a word.

Isaiah answers, 'Be not afraid'; God will send a blast upon the king, who shall hear a rumour and fall by the sword in his own land. Deliverance is promised. God can scatter the mightiest army with a word.

Isaiah answers, 'Be not afraid'; God will send a blast upon the king, who shall hear a rumour and fall by the sword in his own land. Deliverance is promised. God can scatter the mightiest army with a word.

Rabshakeh withdraws, and Sennacherib sends a letter: 'Let not thy God... deceive thee,' trusting Jerusalem will fall like the rest. The blasphemy is repeated in writing. The enemy presses his attack on faith.

Rabshakeh withdraws, and Sennacherib sends a letter: 'Let not thy God... deceive thee,' trusting Jerusalem will fall like the rest. The blasphemy is repeated in writing. The enemy presses his attack on faith.

He boasts of all the lands and kings his fathers destroyed, listing fallen cities and their helpless gods. He measures God by idols. The proud forget the difference between the Maker and the made.
He boasts of all the lands and kings his fathers destroyed, listing fallen cities and their helpless gods. He measures God by idols. The proud forget the difference between the Maker and the made.
He boasts of all the lands and kings his fathers destroyed, listing fallen cities and their helpless gods. He measures God by idols. The proud forget the difference between the Maker and the made.

Hezekiah takes the letter, spreads it before the LORD, and prays, 'O LORD... thou art the God, even thou alone.' He lays the threat before God. The believer's best answer to a hostile letter is to bring it to the throne of grace.

Hezekiah takes the letter, spreads it before the LORD, and prays, 'O LORD... thou art the God, even thou alone.' He lays the threat before God. The believer's best answer to a hostile letter is to bring it to the throne of grace.

He asks God to hear and see, owning that the heathen gods were no gods, and prays, 'save us... that all the kingdoms... may know that thou art the LORD.' He seeks God's glory. Deliverance is asked for the honour of God's name.

He asks God to hear and see, owning that the heathen gods were no gods, and prays, 'save us... that all the kingdoms... may know that thou art the LORD.' He seeks God's glory. Deliverance is asked for the honour of God's name.

Isaiah sends word: 'the virgin... daughter of Zion, hath despised thee'; whom has the Assyrian reproached but 'the Holy One of Israel'? His boasts are answered. To insult God's people is to insult God Himself.

God declares He long ago ordained these events, and because the Assyrian's rage rose up to Him, 'I will put my hook in thy nose,' and turn him back. The proud is but a tool. God leads the raging king home like a beast on a leash.

God declares He long ago ordained these events, and because the Assyrian's rage rose up to Him, 'I will put my hook in thy nose,' and turn him back. The proud is but a tool. God leads the raging king home like a beast on a leash.

God declares He long ago ordained these events, and because the Assyrian's rage rose up to Him, 'I will put my hook in thy nose,' and turn him back. The proud is but a tool. God leads the raging king home like a beast on a leash.
The sign to Hezekiah: this year and next, food grows of itself, then sowing returns, and 'the remnant... shall again take root.' Provision is promised. God assures His people of survival and renewal.
The sign to Hezekiah: this year and next, food grows of itself, then sowing returns, and 'the remnant... shall again take root.' Provision is promised. God assures His people of survival and renewal.
The sign to Hezekiah: this year and next, food grows of itself, then sowing returns, and 'the remnant... shall again take root.' Provision is promised. God assures His people of survival and renewal.

The Assyrian 'shall not come into this city,' for God will defend it 'for mine own sake, and for my servant David's sake.' Zion is safe. God guards His people for His own glory and His covenant promise.

The Assyrian 'shall not come into this city,' for God will defend it 'for mine own sake, and for my servant David's sake.' Zion is safe. God guards His people for His own glory and His covenant promise.

The Assyrian 'shall not come into this city,' for God will defend it 'for mine own sake, and for my servant David's sake.' Zion is safe. God guards His people for His own glory and His covenant promise.

The angel of the LORD smote 185,000 in the camp; Sennacherib went home and was slain by his own sons as he worshipped his idol. The boaster is undone. The God he mocked answered, and the false god he served could not save him.
